title: Iran’s former crown prince on its potential path to democracy

transcript.text: I don't call it a protest anymore. This
is a revolution. People have had it with
this regime. Now is an opportunity to
put an end to this misery. And this
regime has proven that is willing to
massacre thousands of people just to
keep themselves in power for the whole
world to see. I wish the world was
witness to what has happened in the last
72 hours. The number of victims and the
way they have done it. We're talking
about a conflict where people are
getting butchered and massacred and they
have no means to defend themselves and
an intervention ob foreign uh
governments that can come and help us to
push back about this atrocity is needed.
>> Do you see yourself as a as more of a
unifying figurehead or do you see
yourself actually wielding power? I'm
talking about this phase of transition
being the only person that people can
trust as being a neutral or
>> you're going to run the thing.
>> I have offered my services in that
capacity and I believe the trust that
people have given to me behooves me to
respond to that call and say I accept
that uh uh responsibility. I'm not
working alone obviously. I'm trying to
bring as many people who are like-minded
in on on the basis of three very simple
principles. Number one is Iran
territorial integrity. The second
principle that we invoke a clear
separation of religion from state. Not
to have yet again a dominant single
ideology. And the most important point a
guarantee of self-determination by means
of free elections. And that's ultimately
what leads us to a democratic
alternative. Now my role in all this is
to make sure that a at least these core
principles are achieved. B that the
temporary government enables the nation
to have several options of electing the
representative in a constitutional
assembly to ultimately determine the
form and shape that such future secular
democracy will take. It's a process and
the minute the ultimate will of the
people is ratified by means of a final
referendum the temporary government will
transfer power to that elected
parliament and new government. But just
to be very clear that that period will
take a while. In that period you will
rule by decree.
>> This is not a process where a temporary
government gets elected. It is a
voluntary step taken by people like
myself and whoever I'm working with
which of course the endorsement and the
support of the people. I'm not doing
this against their will. I'm doing this
at their ask. That's the difference. So
the ask itself means that they want this
to happen and they want to be able to
trust someone and that that person at
this point is me to do that for them.
And as I said, I'm not doing this.
>> Well, there are there are many people
you have you have a number of and a
reasonably large fraction of the Iranian
population is are fervent supporters of
yours, but not everybody is. Not
everybody is. What is the legitimacy
that you are using to do this? I mean,
you know, one could say you're coming
in, you've spent your entire life since
you were a teenager outside the country.
Your father was not exactly a popular
figure and now you're coming in and
saying, "Right, I'm here in charge
again, and this is what I'm going to
do."
>> I'm only responding to what people are
asking for.
What name has been chanted in the
streets of Iran in the last few months?
What name do you see be written on the
walls? What slogans have they chanted in
the recent weeks? They have chanted the
name of my family. They have called for
me specifically.
I think that's pretty much of an
indication of what the people want.
>> That's I guess that's what I'm asking.
Do you think that I mean yes, there have
clearly been chances.
>> Is that sufficient to establish?
>> It's not everybody, right?
>> Of course it's not. Do can you tell me
anywhere in the world that there
somebody has a 100% vote of everybody?
Of course not.
>> But the majority believe in that
direction. It's not even an issue of the
final form. Republicans, monarchies,
that's not even the debate. I'm not here
to advocate an outcome, but I'm a bridge
to that destination. I think one of the
things that we've learned and learned
particularly in the Middle East that it
is quite hard to build democracies and
particularly when you have power vacuums
and when you have had decades of
repression. When you look at that
history, what have you learned from
other countries about things that you'd
like to avoid to ensure that this
transition that you're laying out will
be successful? First of all, do not
underestimate
the intelligence and the knowledge that
my fellow Iranians have inside Iran.
Despite the fact of the brainwashing
that went into their heads, they are
extremely smart when it comes to these
matters. Just if you were to be able to
talk to today's Gen Z in Iran, they are
way ahead of what you think,
as you just described, it is hard to do
it in the Middle East. Number one.
Number two, today we have Iranians that
are third generation when their parents
had to leave Iran in the late '7s as a
result of revolution or shortly after.
They now reside and have are citizens of
countries like Sweden, like the United
States, like New Zealand and Australia,
like Japan. Many of these countries
known democracies, functioning
democracies. And as citizens of those
countries, they have learned how to
assimilate, adapt, and learn in practice
all the value systems of these countries
that have a democratic culture.
>> I don't doubt all of the history and
capabilities and the power of the
diaspora. But there are also a very
large number of people within Iran who
rely essentially financially on this
regime. There are a lot of people who
are essentially they and their families
doing very well corrupt or not corrupt
out of this regime. What is it that you
will do to ensure that you have those
people on side that you don't wreck the
institutions of the country but that you
do consolidate the change?
>> Look, uh I'm not sure I totally agree
with what you just said that it's not so
overwhelmingly widespread those who
benefit from this regime. I still think
that this is a very ruling corrupt mafia
that basically only distribute shares
among themselves while the nation is
starving. Look, we're talking about
devaluation of our currency at levels
that are just unfathomable.
You're talking about the rate of
inflation. You're talking about
the levels of uh the poverty line in
Iran. This is for a nation that in terms
of it resource
>> 20 people, right? It's 10,000 people
that are attached to the regime. It's
not it's not a healthy system. It's not
a healthy economy. And the potentials
that exist in Iran, even for those who
you might say,
>> think that they somehow benefit from the
status quo, they could be 10 times
richer,
when you have a healthy economy, when
you have transparency, when you have
accountability, when the banking system
operates under the same norms and
regulations of standard that are
acceptable in the world, which they
don't. There's so many factors that
would contribute to even a better
opportunity for those who are already
wealthy. Well, they can invest in their
own country and we're not asking them to
stop uh you know uh progressing. But the
difference is for the masses. The
difference is for areas of the country
that are totally impoverished.
>> Much of this will depend on sanctions
relief and the integration of the
Iranian economy. So, can you stay
clearly that you would presumably would
you give up on Iran's nuclear program
straight away and in order to get
sanctions?
>> Yes, we don't need that. We simply don't
need that.
>> You're very close to Israel. You're very
pro-Israel. You you backed Israel in the
bombing of Tran last year, which was I
think quite controversial even amongst
your fellow countrymen. Tell me what
your relationship would be with Israel.
I think if Iranians thought that this is
bad for Iran, you wouldn't see a single
Israeli flag fly next to an Iranian
flag. In every demonstration we've seen
outside of Iran against Islamic regime.
There are only two countries on this
planet that can claim that they have a
biblical relationship with one another.
It's Iran and Israel. When Cyrus the
Great freed
the slaves in Babylon to return to their
homes and rebuild their temple in
Jerusalem. That's where it all started.
And today the childrens of Cyrus know
that this time it is Israel that comes
to the rescue. And we can have a very
strong bond in the 21st century, not 25
centuries ago, as the best possible
strategic partners
to work together and our Arab neighbors
to bring about stability and peace,
which is the first element that the
whole world ought to want. And we work
together as brothers. We work together
as committed to the same common interest
that we should all have and we do.
Otherwise, the Abraham Accord had no leg
to stand on.
